🚨 Acaba de confirmarse: un nuevo fallo en el núcleo de Linux llamado Bad Epoll (CVE-2026-46242) permite a un usuario no privilegiado obtener acceso de root en máquinas Linux y Android.  El fallo, identificado como CVE-2026-46242, afecta a kernels Linux 6.4+ y se debe a una condición de carrera *use-after-free* en el subsistema eventpoll/epoll. Esto permite a un atacante explotar la vulnerabilidad de forma fiable (~99% de éxito) desde el sandbox de Chrome y potencialmente en Android.  La vulnerabilidad puede ser explotada por usuarios sin privilegios, lo que significa que cualquier usuario con acceso a la máquina puede obtener acceso de root. Esto puede tener consecuencias graves, como la pérdida de control sobre la máquina y el acceso a datos sensibles.  Ya se ha publicado una solución para este problema, por lo que es importante que los administradores de sistemas y los dueños de dispositivos Android actualicen sus sistemas lo antes posible. ¿Estás en riesgo? Revisa esto: asegúrate de actualizar tu kernel Linux y dispositivo Android con la última versión disponible.  #Ciberseguridad #CVE #SeguridadDigital #PYMEsMX thehackernews.com/2026/07/ne…

Pre-auth memory overread vulnerabilities in edge infrastructure like Citrix NetScaler are especially concerning because they may expose sensitive information before authentication even happens. For organizations relying on NetScaler ADC or Gateway, this kind of risk can impact remote access, identity security, session protection, and overall business continuity.  The lesson is clear: internet-facing systems need continuous attention—not occasional review.  Organizations should prioritize:  ✅ Identify all Citrix NetScaler ADC and Gateway appliances   ✅ Review vendor guidance and affected versions immediately   ✅ Apply patches or mitigations as soon as available   ✅ Rotate potentially exposed credentials and session tokens   ✅ Review authentication, VPN, and administrative access logs   ✅ Restrict unnecessary external exposure   ✅ Monitor for suspicious access patterns and data leakage indicators   ✅ Validate incident response and recovery procedures    With Vistem Elevate powered by VistemSecurePro, Vistem Solutions helps businesses move from reactive patching to proactive cyber resilience through vCISO leadership, vulnerability management, identity protection, threat monitoring, and measurable security outcomes. 🚨 CRITICAL CYBER INTELLIGENCE ALERT: GOVERNMENT INFRASTRUCTURE - AFGHANISTAN 🇦🦺  [STATUS: ALLEGED / UNCONFIRMED / STRONG EVIDENCE / SOURCE: THREAT EXPOSURE CHANNELS / DATE: JULY 4, 2026]  THE "ERRESIRA" GROUP CLAIMS COMPLETE COMPROMISE AND EXFILTRATION OF 1.5 TB OF DATA FROM OVER 18 GOVERNMENT PORTALS  Through passive monitoring of threat actor communities and data leaks, a cyber espionage post targeting Afghanistan's state IT infrastructure has been intercepted. According to press releases and collected forensic evidence, the cyber operations group known as Erresira claims to have concluded a months-long intrusion campaign that allowed them to extract a massive 1.5 TB backup of government data. The data being auctioned compromises the source code, perimeter cryptography, and internal messaging systems of multiple ministries, with a heavy focus on the Ministry of Finance.  🏢 Allegedly Affected Entities: More than 18 Afghan government websites and portals, with a massive direct impact on the Ministry of Finance directories (mof.gov.af).  👤 Threat Actor: Erresira  ⚔️ Reported Breach Volume: 1.5 Terabytes of data encompassing more than 128,000 folders and 2.2 million files.  🔍 Verification Status: UNCONFIRMED BY GOVERNMENT AUTHORITIES. As of July 4, 2026, no Afghan technology or defense entity has issued statements confirming the loss of integrity of its data centers. Nevertheless, the alert is being processed under the criteria of Maximum Severity and Critical Geopolitical Impact: the captured directory trees detail mail server structures (dovecot.index, cPanel configurations), NGINX web server logs updated to April 2026 (smtp.mof.gov.af-ssl_log-Apr-2026_NGINX.gz), and government email accounts associated with active institutional domains.  🗂️ FORENSIC ANALYSIS AND EXFILTRATION STRUCTURE  The technical documentation provided by the perpetrator and the logical trees presented demonstrate that the criminal group allegedly compromised the persistence layer and perimeter security configuration of the entire state web ecosystem:  📬 1. The Correspondence Directory of the Ministry of Finance This module represents a massive leak of internal intelligence, with 2 million files and 105,000 folders compromised:  Institutional Content Leak: Inclusion of Base64-encoded text strings containing confidential documents and files (TXT, CVE, ZIP, XLSX, RAR formats).  Data Filters: The exfiltrated files range from direct complaints from clients and citizens to the personnel files and data of those directly responsible for the management of the Ministry of Finance. The mailbox dumps visible in the screenshots detail mailboxes with full storage bins (Sent, Drafts, Archive, Junk, Spam) and indexes.  🔑 2. Cryptographic Keys and Portal Source Code  The most dangerous technical aspect of Erresira's announcement lies in the compromise of the internal infrastructure that allows control of the portals:  Environment Secrets Leakage: Extraction of all .env configuration files from the ministries' web servers, which commonly store database passwords, API keys, and debug configurations in plain text.  SSL Cryptographic Compromise: Theft of cryptographic private keys and RSA SSL certificate keys, enabling site impersonation or network traffic decryption.  Intellectual Property Loss: Exfiltration of DNS zone files, general server configurations, and the complete source code of all 18 affected government websites (including Laravel scripts, Node.js configurations, composer.json, package.json, and backend dependencies).  🛡️ PREVENTIVE TECHNICAL RECOMMENDATIONS FOR CONTAINMENT (CISOs / GLOBAL SOC)  Given the full extent of the infrastructure compromise claimed by Erresira, the network engineering teams of the affected ministries are advised to immediately deploy the following disaster mitigation measures:  🛑 Global SSL Certificate Revocation and Private Key Rotation: Priority and urgency, invalidate all SSL certificates linked to gov.af subdomains with global Certificate Authorities (CAs) and generate new cryptographic key pairs. Assume that any RSA keys prior to July 2026 are fully exposed.  🔑 Purge and Change Passwords in Environment Files (.env): Immediately change the passwords for all production databases, API keys, SSH/FTP access, and email credentials that were mapped in the .env files of the exfiltrated web applications.  📊 MONITORING AND EVALUATION  Intelligence System: analyzer.vecert.io  Inspection System URL: monitor.vecert.io #CyberSecurity #Afghanistan #Erresira #MinistryOfFinance #DataLeak #GovAf #SSLBreach #RSAKeys #SourceCodeLeak #DovecotIndex #NginxLogs #EnvFiles #ThreatIntelligence #CyberAlert #VECERT #Infosec #UnverifiedIncident

HTB DevArea (retired): a four-layer Linux attack chain walks through CVE-2022-46364 arbitrary file read, Hoverfly CVE-2025-54123 command injection, Flask cookie forgery, and a symlink-chain bypass in a sudo script.  - CVE-2022-46364 (Apache CXF before 3.4.10/3.5.5) lets an attacker embed an xop:Include href="file:///..." element inside a multipart/related MTOM SOAP request. The server fetches the path, base64-encodes the bytes, and reflects them in the response. The employee-service.jar on the anonymous FTP server fingered the vulnerable CXF 3.2.14 version before a packet was sent. Reading /proc/self/cmdline and /proc/[pid]/cmdline exposed the Hoverfly process command line in plaintext, leaking creds: admin:O7IJ27MyyXiU.  - CVE-2025-54123 (Hoverfly 1.11.3): a PUT to /api/v2/hoverfly/middleware with JSON body {"binary":"/bin/bash","script":"..."} passes user-controlled values directly to exec.Command without sanitization. The server runs the script as the service user, giving a shell as dev_ryan. The fix in 1.12.0 disables the middleware API by default.  - The SysWatch Flask app stores its SECRET_KEY in /etc/syswatch.env with chmod 755 (world-readable). Reading that file via the CXF file-read lets an attacker forge a valid Flask session cookie (flask-unsign or Flask's own SecureCookieSessionInterface).  #DFIR_Radar

To Letters @globeandmail @acoyne @SylviaStead    Under a classified spy satellite, property of the Government of Canada, I learned a few things.  The satellite can view places on Earth at subcentimer resolution through roofs with clarity. Thus, it is able to look through roofs to read ordinarily-sized text on paper.  The satellite is at risk of cyberattack, and indeed it came to be so afflicted. The cyberattack, I learned, commenced approximately 25 years ago, and targeted me (as much as all of Canada) as I lived, worked, and traveled around three continents (North America, Europe, and Africa), entirely without cognizance that I was being watched from space all during.  The satellite's governing policy regime is undemocratic and contrary to international law. Public reason is suppressed by the law which requires it to be classified, like other technological assets in foreign intelligence programs. Genuine elections are in failure, freedom of expression is denied, and Parliament is disenfranchised therefore.  I also learned that conspiracy theory is a term popularized by the CIA for propaganda purposes, by reading in the book "Conspiracy Theory in America" by Professor Lance DeHaven-Smith. This fact, granted, I learnt quite incidentally to endeavoring to learn the facts surrounding the satellite hack.   I learnt in that context that the unethical and torturous (in international legal terms) American mind control research programs continue today, and the 25 years of illicit satellite monitoring to which I referred were purposeful therein. This represents a crime against humanity, and it continues to occur.  The CIA conducts the mind control research, conducts covert action such that the Syrian war was tantamount to a US war of aggression (like many other conflicts, unbeknownst to the public), and even contains a lurking hate group which can explain the findings of the jury in the Dr. Martin Luther King civil trial verdict, of a murderous conspiracy with US governmental involvement.  Did a secret hate group within the CIA kill John F. Kennedy? And are many UFO/UAP sightings explainable in terms of nefarious activity from Area 51? The answer to both questions is more likely yes than no.  So, I continue to learn.

Today, I officially apologize to Pump Club and to everyone who may have been affected by this. This was not Pump Club. The matter with Pump Club is closed for us. After a thorough investigation, we have identified who was actually behind the hacking and deletion of Lucy’s fan page. It was a woman who had been a very close person to our family for a long time. We did not expect this at all. It was a stab in the back from someone we trusted — driven by black envy and revenge. We have already identified the person responsible. The attack was carried out from Kyrgyzstan, from Bishkek. In response, we removed this woman from our friends list, blocked her, and also filed a complaint against her on Facebook. Just as she did to us, we did the same to her. We do not want war and we are not looking for further conflict. We simply want to be left in peace — especially now, as Lucy has an important medical commission for the children on July 10. Once again, I offer my official apologies to Pump Club. The GitHub OpenCTI-Platform/opencti repository is an open-source platform designed to structure, store, organise and visualise cyber threat intelligence (ICT) and its observables.   📋 His Main characteristics:  Standardised modelling: Data structuring based on STIX2 standards.   Enriched data: Linking technical (TTPs, observables) and non-technical (attribution, victimology) elements to their initial sources.   Modern architecture: Web application with a GraphQL API and a user experience-centred interface.   Connected ecosystem: Native integration with other security tools such as MISP, TheHive and the MITRE ATT&CK framework.   🔄 his Knowledge management : Automatic inference:  Automatic generation of new relationships from existing data to facilitate analysis.   Import / Export: Support for multiple data exchange formats, such as CSV and STIX2 bundles.   ⚙️ Deployment and Editions:  Two editions: Available in Community version (free under Apache 2.0 license) and in Enterprise version (advanced features published by Filigran).   Flexible installation: Deployment supported via Docker, Helm charts, or manually.  github.com/opencti-platform/…

🤖 Malwoverview — Multi-Source Threat Hunting & Malware Intelligence CLI  Malwoverview is an open-source Python-based threat hunting and malware triage platform that acts as a unified client for numerous malware intelligence, sandbox, IOC, vulnerability, and reputation services. Rather than operating as a standalone sandbox, it aggregates intelligence from platforms such as VirusTotal, Hybrid Analysis, Malware Bazaar, Triage, ThreatFox, URLHaus, AlienVault OTX, Shodan, GreyNoise, AbuseIPDB, Malpedia, VulnCheck, URLScan[.]io, and others, allowing security analysts to investigate malware samples, hashes, URLs, IP addresses, domains, CVEs, and Indicators of Compromise from a single command-line interface. Malwoverview supports static and dynamic malware analysis, cross-platform sample submission and retrieval, malware family identification, YARA scanning, IOC extraction, MITRE ATT&CK mapping, directory triage, APK analysis through Android Debug Bridge (ADB), CVE and vulnerability research, reputation lookups, WHOIS and RDAP queries, Shodan enrichment, BGP intelligence, and cross-service hash correlation. The tool includes structured JSON and CSV output, an interactive REPL, a Text User Interface (TUI), SQLite-based result caching, proxy support, batch processing, and automated directory analysis for large-scale investigations. Recent releases also introduce AI-powered threat enrichment through Claude, Gemini, OpenAI, and Ollama, enabling automated malware risk assessments, MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping, malware family identification, and analyst recommendations while allowing privacy-conscious users to perform local inference with Ollama. Designed for malware analysts, incident responders, SOC teams, digital forensics practitioners, and cyber threat intelligence professionals, Malwoverview accelerates first-response investigations by consolidating dozens of threat intelligence services into a single extensible analysis workflow without automatically uploading malware samples unless explicitly instructed by the analyst.  🔗 github.com/alexandreborges/m… #CyberSecurity #ThreatIntelligence #MalwareAnalysis #ThreatHunting #DFIR #SOC #IOC #MITREATTACK #OpenSource #Python

日本のホテル業界を狙い、C2サーバーの所在地をブロックチェーン上のスマートコントラクトから取得する新型マルウェア「TONResolver」が出回っています。Booking[.]comを装い、提携ホテル宛てに送られる偽の宿泊者クレーム通知メールを通じて、5月下旬から配布されているとのことです。  【要点の整理】  ・Trend Microが確認したもので、C2サーバーのアドレスをThe Open Network（TON）ブロックチェーン上のスマートコントラクトに保存する「デッドドロップリゾルバー」方式を採用。この手口自体はMITRE ATT&CKでも定義された既知の手法（T1102.001）だが、保存先にTONブロックチェーンを使う点が特徴で、攻撃者はコントラクトを書き換えるだけでマルウェア本体を改変せずにC2サーバーを切り替えられる仕組み。  ・配布経路は「重要：ゲスト滞在レビュー依頼」といった件名の一斉フィッシングと、無害な問い合わせから始めて信頼関係を築いた後に不正リンクを送る「会話型攻撃」の2種類。TONResolver自体は端末から直接ファイルや認証情報を窃取する機能を持たず、初期アクセスとコマンド実行の足場という位置づけ。ただし確立されたKeepalive通信を通じて攻撃者が標的を選別し追加コマンドを送れるため、実際にMDRの観測ではブラウザ（Chrome・Edge）の認証情報やCookie、閲覧履歴を狙う後続の実行ファイル投下とlsass[.]exeの関与を確認。  ・感染チェーンは、写真を装うショートカットファイルの実行から始まり、PowerShellスクリプトが正規のnodejs[.]orgからNode[.]jsをダウンロードして実行環境として利用する構成。全通信はWebSocket上でECDH（secp256k1）鍵交換とHKDF-SHA256による鍵導出を経て、AES-256-CBCで暗号化される仕組み。  対策としては、C2解決に使われるtonapi[.]ioへの接続制限や、AppDataパス配下で動作するNode[.]jsプロセスの監視が挙げられています。本キャンペーンは継続中で、感染端末はKeepaliveで攻撃者からのコマンド受信を待ち続けるため、標的に選ばれればいつでも攻撃が進行しうるリスクが持続しているとしています。  詳細は以下を参照： trendmicro.com/en_us/researc…

TACHI - Threat Modeling and Vulnerability Detection Harness for Claude Code  tachi is a threat modeling and AI-reasoning vulnerability detection harness for Claude Code:  * SAST catches syntax-level bugs * the harness reasons over your architecture description to catch logic-level ones — broken authentication flows, missing privilege boundaries, prompt injection paths, agent autonomy gaps, cross-layer attack chains.  It runs as a new scanning column alongside SAST / SCA / Secrets, with two views of one engine:  * Threat modeling view — structured artifacts: threats.md, SARIF, narrative report, attack trees, MAESTRO classification. * Vulnerability scanning view — per-finding logic-level risks surfaced from the architecture, scored, and mapped to OWASP / MITRE ATT&CK / ATLAS / NIST AI RMF / CWE.  One command (/tachi.threat-model) dispatches 14 specialized agents and produces a complete threat model. Five post-pipeline commands enrich the results:  * /tachi.risk-score for quantitative scoring * /tachi.compensating-controls for codebase control analysis * /tachi.infographic for visual risk diagrams * /tachi.security-report for a professional PDF assessment booklet * /tachi.architecture for automated architecture description generation.  Works with any stack: the harness analyzes architecture, not code  Repo: github.com/davidmatousek/tac… #TrustEverybodyButCutTheCards

Mac cleanup starts with one question: what keeps coming back?  MacPersistenceChecker is an open-source macOS app for finding and reviewing persistence mechanisms on your Mac.  It helps you see what runs automatically by scanning common autostart locations, checking signatures and behavior, then giving you timelines, risk scores, and context for what to keep, watch, or remove.  Key features:  • 22+ persistence mechanisms – scans LaunchAgents, LaunchDaemons, login items, extensions, profiles, cron jobs, shell startup files, TCC/Accessibility, and more • Code-signature + risk checks – flags unsigned or ad-hoc signed binaries, suspicious paths, risky entitlements, LOLBin patterns, and intent mismatches • Timeline + snapshot diffing – track first seen, created, modified, and executed timestamps, then compare changes after installs • Local-first privacy model – scanning, scoring, and graph reasoning run locally; optional Claude analysis uses your own Anthropic key • Investigation outputs – includes MITRE ATT&CK mapping, MCP server tools, forensic JSON export, and containment actions  It’s open-source (MIT license).  Link in the reply 👇

🚨 ICYMI, Anthropic just published a 36-page security guide that basically tells you to stop trusting your own AI agents.  If you run agents on Claude Code, MCP servers, or automation tools, pay attention.  The attack timeline has collapsed.  AI models compress the gap between a vulnerability and a working exploit from months to hours, for mere dollars.  Agents introduce new autonomous risks, from tool poisoning to context memory manipulation.  The most useful idea in the guide is Anthropic's new security test:  Does a control make an attack impossible, or just tedious?  Automated attackers have unlimited patience.  They will grind straight through friction like rate limits and 2FA.  To defend at the speed of AI, you need hard barriers and automated defensive operations.  Here is how Anthropic says you should lock down agents: → Treat static API keys as compromised. Use short-lived tokens that expire in minutes. → Apply "Least Agency": explicitly limit what each tool can DO. → Sandbox agents that process untrusted inputs like emails and web pages. → Scope permissions dynamically per task, not permanently.  I've added the link to the guide in the 🧵↓

sample_tweets.text: 17 years ago today, someone pointed a botnet at the White House website on the Fourth of July. On purpose. For the symbolism.  That's the detail that stuck with analysts even after the technical postmortems were done. Whoever designed the July 4th, 2009 DDoS campaign against US and South Korean government sites understood American political culture well enough to pick that weekend deliberately. That's not a script kiddie move.  Here's what actually happened. Starting July 4th, a botnet of roughly 20,000 compromised machines began flooding servers across both countries. The target list read like a civics textbook: the White House, the Pentagon, the NSA, DHS, the State Department, the NYSE, NASDAQ. On the South Korean side: the Blue House presidential complex, major banks, government ministries. The Washington Post and Amazon got hit too, probably for reach and visibility. Twenty-one sites total, using over 400 exploited servers worldwide, with about 12,000 of the infected machines sitting right there in South Korea.  The traffic volume was actually modest by DDoS standards. Jose Nazario, who was tracking it at the time, clocked it at around 23 megabits per second. Not catastrophic numbers. But the attacks kept going. Sustained, methodical, day after day through the holiday weekend. Several sites stayed degraded for days. The goal wasn't to crater infrastructure permanently. It was to demonstrate reach, test response times, and make a point.  North Korea had been having a busy summer. They ran missile tests in the days surrounding the attacks and were operating under fresh UN sanctions, imposed less than a month earlier after their second nuclear test in May 2009. Seoul's National Intelligence Service came out fairly quickly saying the attacks appeared to have been "elaborately prepared and staged by a certain organization or state." That phrasing was diplomatic. The attribution picture would get clearer over time.  Later analysis pointed to Lab 110, a North Korean cyber unit focused on intelligence collection and attacks against command systems. Lab 110 operates under Unit 121, which is the organizational umbrella for Pyongyang's main offensive cyber apparatus. The same actors were assessed with 95 percent confidence to be behind a second wave of attacks in March 2011, though that campaign fizzled faster because defenders had learned from 2009 and moved quicker to clean infected systems.  McAfee's retrospective framing was useful: they called it "an armed cyber reconnaissance operation." The point wasn't to destroy. It was to probe. See how fast US and South Korean defenders could respond. See which systems buckled. Map the terrain.  The operational security details are what dated this as state-level work. The botnet was programmed to self-destruct after 10 days, systematically overwriting files to prevent forensic recovery. Source files deleted automatically. Hard disks on compromised machines wiped. Someone put real effort into making attribution difficult and evidence scarce.  What this operation changed, or at least accelerated, is the seriousness with which both governments treated cyber capabilities as an actual strategic instrument rather than a nuisance category. Before 2009, a lot of the official conversation treated DDoS attacks as hacktivism or low-end criminal behavior. Hitting the Pentagon and the NSA on Independence Day, in a coordinated two-country operation, with built-in forensic countermeasures, forced a category upgrade.  North Korea got increasingly good at this. The 2014 Sony hack, the 2016 Bangladesh Bank heist, the WannaCry ransomware deployment in 2017, the sustained cryptocurrency theft operations that have since pulled in billions. The cyber program became one of Pyongyang's primary tools for generating hard currency, conducting espionage, and occasionally just causing chaos for political effect. The July 2009 campaign was early-stage compared to all that. But the logic was already there: use cheap asymmetric tools to punch at adversaries who would crush you in a conventional fight.  The botnet from 2009 is long dead, self-destructed on schedule. Lab 110 is still very much operational.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #CoordinatedCyberWarfare #DDoSAttacks #InfrastructureAttacks

Could Hemi's Architecture Have Prevented the Ronin Bridge Hack?  In March 2022, North Korea's Lazarus Group stole $624 million from Ronin bridge  not by breaking cryptography, but by compromising five of its nine validator private keys. Four belonged to Sky Mavis's own servers. A fifth was accessible through a gas-free RPC node whose permissions were never revoked after a November 2021 integration. Two transactions later, 173,600 ETH and 25.5 million USDC were gone, and the theft went undetected for six full days.  The root cause wasn't a sophisticated exploit. It was that five human-controlled keys held absolute authority over the entire bridge. Steal those keys, approve any withdrawal you want.  @hemi_xyz 's tunnel architecture removes that attack vector at the design level. There are no validator private keys authorizing withdrawals. Settlement instead depends on Bitcoin's Proof of Proof consensus  Hemi's state is embedded directly into Bitcoin blocks, so release of funds requires confirming against Bitcoin's own proof of work, not a signing group's approval. An attacker mimicking the Lazarus playbook would find no keys to steal, because keys aren't the gatekeeper.  This wouldn't eliminate all risk  tunnel contracts are still software with their own audit requirements. But the specific vector that cost Ronin $624 million simply doesn't exist in a system where Bitcoin's hash power, not a multisig, decides finality.

The SCIF @TheSCIF A-WEB：国連が支援する「選挙不正カルテル」  A-WEBは韓国に本部を置く世界最大の選挙関連機関ですが、NEC（韓国中央選挙管理委員会）システムのハッキング被害、Miru Systems（ミル・システムズ）社の技術的トラブル、イラクでの70%・コンゴ民主共和国（DRC）での45%という機器不具合率、さらには「幽霊有権者」、偽造投票用紙、世界各地で見られる不審なパターンなどとの関わりが指摘されています。 1.) 韓国中央選挙管理委員会（NEC）におけるサイバーセキュリティの不備：  - 2023年7月～9月に行われたNIS（国家情報院）とKISA（韓国インターネット振興院）による合同監査（結果は2023年10月10日公表）において、NECのシステムに重大な脆弱性があることが判明。 - 有権者登録システムへの侵入により、「幽霊有権者」の作成や、期日前投票を行った有権者を「未投票」として処理することが可能な状態だった。 - 投票用紙印刷システムにおいて、公式印章ファイルの盗用や、正規のQRコードを模倣した偽造投票用紙の作成が可能という脆弱性があった。 - 開票システムが、不正なUSBデバイスや無線接続を介して改ざんされる恐れがあった。 - ネットワーク分離が不十分、パスワードが推測されやすい、機密データ・候補者リスト・在外有権者名簿が平文（暗号化なし）で保存されているなどの問題があった。 - NECは自らのセキュリティを「100点満点中100点」と評価していたが、独立した再評価では「31.5点」という結果だった。 - NECはセキュリティ評価に不適格な業者を利用しており、これは韓国法に違反していた。 - 過去2年間でNECは8回のサイバー攻撃を受けており、そのうち7回は北朝鮮のハッカー集団「ラザルス・グループ（Lazarus Group）」によるものだった。 - 2021年4月、NECのインターネット接続用PCが北朝鮮のハッカー集団「キムスキー（Kimsuky）」のマルウェアに感染し、機密文書が流出した。 - NISから報告された北朝鮮による複数のハッキング事案について、NECは「事前の認識がなかった」。 2.) NECの主要技術ベンダーであるMiru Systems社の問題点：  - Miru Systems社（韓国・城南市）は、韓国の電子投票・開票システムの保守・修理を行うとともに、同システムを海外へ輸出している。 - イラクでは70%、コンゴ民主共和国では45%という高い機器不具合率を記録した。 - Miru Systems社のシステムを導入した国々（イラク、DRC、フィリピン、キルギス、エルサルバドル、エクアドル、ブルガリアなど）では、導入後に深刻な不正疑惑や抗議活動が発生したと報じられている。 - 2009年以降、バウマン・モスクワ国立工科大学を介したロシアの選挙システムとの長期的な関わり（2024年のロシアの選挙を含む）。 - 2017年、イラクへの開票機輸出に関する1億ドル規模の契約。  - ミシガン大学のアレックス・ハルダーマン教授：「Miru社のこれまでの実績は深刻な懸念を抱かせるものだ……」  - Politico（ポリティコ）の調査により、Miru社の実績について「長きにわたり、懸念すべきものであり、かつ詳細に記録されている」と指摘する専門家の声が浮き彫りになった。  2024年の議会選挙において、期日前投票の結果がデジタル操作され、野党に有利な結果になったとの疑惑が浮上。尹錫悦（ユン・ソンニョル）大統領は選挙不正などを理由に戒厳令を宣言し、軍が一時、中央選挙管理委員会（NEC）本部を占拠した。その後、尹氏は2026年2月に「内乱罪」で終身刑を言い渡された。  期日前投票と当日投票の間に統計的な乖離が継続的に見られ、研究者のコン・ビョンホ博士はこれを「一貫した操作パターン」であると指摘した。  2025年6月3日の大統領選挙では、リベラル派のイ・ジェミョン（李在明）候補が49.4%の得票率で「勝利」した。モース・H・タン元米国大使、ジョン・R・ミルズ退役大佐、ブラッドリー・A・セイヤー博士、グラント・ニューシャム退役大佐らを含む国際選挙監視団（IEMT）は、以下の点を報告した：  - 期日前投票と当日投票の間に大きな統計的乖離がある。 - 電子集計システムに関するセキュリティおよび透明性の懸念。 - 投票用紙の取り扱いおよび管理の連続性（チェーン・オブ・カストディ）における不備。 - 市民監視員の活動妨害およびNECの協力不足。 2026年6月3日の地方選挙では、14,288カ所の投票所のうち50カ所で投票用紙が不足し、22カ所で投票が滞った。NECのノ・テアク（盧泰嶽）委員長が辞任し、警察がNEC本部を家宅捜索した。 A-WEB = 111カ国・121の選挙管理機関で構成される組織。2013年設立、韓国・仁川（インチョン）の松島（ソンド）​​に本部を置く。USAID（米国国際開発庁）、IFES、Democracy International、NDI、IRIなどとMOU（覚書）を締結（2014年）。 A-WEBは「国連支援の選挙システム・カルテル」として機能しており、USAIDが選挙資金を拠出する発展途上国に対し、脆弱性が指摘されるMiru社のシステムを含む韓国の選挙手法や技術を輸出している。別名：体制転換（レジーム・チェンジ）工作の一環。 A-WEBは2016年および2020年の米国選挙において、現地での選挙監視活動を行った。バーチャル開催。詳細な公開報告書は発表されていない。米国の法曹関係者シドニー・パウエル氏は、A-WEBの海外での活動について司法省（DOJ）による調査を求めた。同氏はA-WEBを、韓国の水原（スウォン）に活動の拠点を置くとされる「選挙問題に関する国際的ネットワーク」であると指摘している。 A-WEBは、イラクやコンゴ民主共和国（DRC）などで行われる、米国の納税者の資金が投入された選挙におけるリスクの中心に位置している。A-WEBは、世界各地での政権交代工作に関与する主要な資金提供機関（USAIDとのつながりを持つ）であり、選挙操作の手段に対して資金を供給し、それらを組織化している。これは、世界中に共産主義を拡散させる大規模な工作活動の一環である。 資料：選挙犯罪局（Election Crime Bureau） A-WEB：国連が支援する「選挙不正カルテル」  A-WEBは韓国に本部を置く世界最大の選挙関連機関であるが、NEC（韓国中央選挙管理委員会）のシステムへのハッキング被害、Miru Tech社によるシステム障害（イラクで70％、DRCで45％の不具合が発生）、架空の有権者、偽造投票用紙、そして世界各地で見られる不審なパターンなどとの関連が指摘されている。 1.) 韓国中央選挙管理委員会（NEC）におけるサイバーセキュリティの不備：  - 202023年7月から9月にかけて実施され、同年10月10日に結果が公表されたNIS（国家情報院）とKISA（韓国インターネット振興院）による合同監査において、NEC（韓国中央選挙管理委員会）のシステムに深刻な脆弱性があることが判明した。 - 有権者登録システムに侵入し、「幽霊有権者」を作成したり、期日前投票を行った有権者を「未投票」として処理したりすることが可能だった。 - 投票用紙印刷システムには、公式印章ファイルの盗用や、正規のQRコードを模倣した偽造投票用紙が作成されるリスクがあった。 - 開票システムは、不正なUSBデバイスや無線接続を通じて改ざんされる恐れがあった。 - ネットワーク分離が不十分であり、パスワードは容易に推測可能なものであったほか、機密データ、候補者リスト、在外有権者名簿などが平文（暗号化なし）で保存されていた。 - NECは自らのセキュリティ体制を100点満点中100点と評価していたが、第三者による再評価では31.5点という結果だった。 - NECはセキュリティ評価に無資格の業者を利用しており、これは韓国法に違反する行為であった。 - NECは過去2年間で8回のサイバー攻撃を受けており、そのうち7回は北朝鮮のハッカー集団「ラザルス・グループ（Lazarus Group）」によるものと特定された。 - 2021年4月、NECのインターネット接続用PCが北朝鮮のハッカー集団「キムスキー（Kimsuky）」のマルウェアに感染し、機密文書が流出した。 - NISから報告された北朝鮮による複数のハッキング事案について、NECは「事前に全く把握していなかった」。 2.) Miru Systems（ミル・システムズ）に関する問題点。同社はNECの主要な技術ベンダーである：  - 韓国・城南（ソンナム）市に拠点を置くMiru Systemsは、韓国の電子投票・開票システムの保守・修理を行うとともに、同システムを海外へ輸出している。 - イラクでは70%、コンゴ民主共和国では45%という高い不具合発生率を記録した。 - Miru Systemsのシステムを導入した国々（イラク、コンゴ民主共和国、フィリピン、キルギス、エルサルバドル、エクアドル、ブルガリアなど）では、導入後に深刻な不正疑惑や抗議活動が発生したと報じられている。 - 2009年以降、バウマン・モスクワ国立工科大学（BMSTU）を介してロシアの選挙システムと長期的な関係があり、これにはロシアの2024年選挙も含まれる。 - 2017年、イラクへの開票機輸出に関する1億ドル規模の契約。  - ミシガン大学のアレックス・ハルダーマン教授：「Miru社のこれまでの実績は深刻な懸念を抱かせるものだ……」  - Politico（ポリティコ）の調査により、Miru社の実績について「長きにわたり、懸念すべきものであり、かつ詳細に記録されている」と指摘する専門家の声が浮き彫りになった。  2024年の議会選挙において、期日前投票の結果がデジタル操作され、野党に有利な結果になったとの疑惑が浮上。尹錫悦（ユン・ソンニョル）大統領は選挙不正などを理由に戒厳令を宣言し、軍が一時、中央選挙管理委員会（NEC）本部を占拠した。その後、尹氏は2026年2月に「内乱罪」で終身刑を言い渡された。  期日前投票と当日投票の間に統計的な乖離が継続的に見られ、研究者のコン・ビョンホ博士はこれを「一貫した操作パターン」であると指摘した。  2025年6月3日の大統領選挙では、リベラル派のイ・ジェミョン（李在明）候補が49.4%の得票率で「勝利」した。モース・H・タン元米国大使、ジョン・R・ミルズ退役大佐、ブラッドリー・A・セイヤー博士、グラント・ニューシャム退役大佐らを含む国際選挙監視団（IEMT）は、以下の点を報告した：  - 期日前投票と当日投票の間に大きな統計的乖離がある。 - 電子集計システムに関するセキュリティおよび透明性の懸念。 - 投票用紙の取り扱いおよび管理の連続性（チェーン・オブ・カストディ）における不備。 - 市民監視員の活動妨害およびNECの協力不足。 2026年6月3日の地方選挙では、14,288カ所の投票所のうち50カ所で投票用紙が不足し、22カ所で投票が滞った。NECのノ・テアク（盧泰嶽）委員長が辞任し、警察がNEC本部を家宅捜索した。 A-WEB = 111カ国・121の選挙管理機関で構成される組織。2013年設立、韓国・仁川（インチョン）の松島（ソンド）​​に本部を置く。USAID（米国国際開発庁）、IFES、Democracy International、NDI、IRIなどとMOU（覚書）を締結（2014年）。 A-WEBは「国連支援の選挙システム・カルテル」として機能しており、USAIDが選挙資金を拠出する発展途上国に対し、脆弱性が指摘されるMiru社のシステムを含む韓国の選挙手法や技術を輸出している。別名：体制転換（レジーム・チェンジ）工作の一環。 A-WEBは2016年および2020年の米国選挙において、現地での選挙監視活動を行った。バーチャル形式で実施され、詳細な公開報告書は発表されていない。米国の法曹関係者シドニー・パウエル氏は、韓国の水原（スウォン）に活動の拠点を置くとされる「選挙問題に関する国際的ネットワーク」について言及し、A-WEBの海外での活動に対する司法省の調査を求めた。 A-WEBは、イラクやコンゴ民主共和国（DRC）などで行われる、米国の納税者の資金で賄われる選挙に関連するリスクの中心に位置している。同団体は、世界各地での政権交代工作における主要な資金提供機関（USAIDとのつながりを持つ）であり、選挙操作の手段に対して資金を供給し、それらを組織化している。これは、世界中に共産主義を拡散させる大規模な工作活動の一環である。 文書：選挙犯罪局（Election Crime Bureau）

sample_texts: APT28 was inside DNC networks in 2016 while most of the U.S. government was still debating whether t

sample_tweets.text: APT28 was inside DNC networks in 2016 while most of the U.S. government was still debating whether to call it an intrusion. GRU Unit 26165. Fancy Bear. Same outfit. CISA's advisory record on Russian state-sponsored threats documents that operation as the cyber spine of a broader interference effort, one where network exploitation and influence operations weren't parallel tracks but a single integrated campaign.  That part got the headlines. The Chinese piece mostly didn't.  CISA and the CSIS significant cyber incidents dataset both record concurrent MSS operations running through the same period. APT-27, tracked under the alias Freelancer, was actively stealing data from U.S. organizations while the country's attention was fixed on Russian election interference. The CSIS dataset covers 2016 through 2023 and the language is unambiguous: APT-27 "acted at the direction of the state." Not freelancing despite the name. Directed.  There's a useful reference point here. In September 2015, the U.S. and China signed an agreement specifically prohibiting economic cyber espionage against each other. Barack Obama and Xi Jinping, Rose Garden, the whole thing. The ink was still relatively fresh in 2016. Chinese actors violated it anyway. The BC Law Review's analysis of the 2016 cyber landscape flags this directly. The agreement existed. The operations continued. Draw your own conclusions about the value of bilateral cyber norms absent enforcement mechanisms.  The attribution problem made all of this worse in real time. The Yale Journal of Law and Technology put it plainly: "agencies may have legitimate reasons for withholding the basis for their attribution." That's a polite way of saying the government knew more than it said, often for months or years after the fact. Intelligence agencies were privately attributing operations with high confidence while public statements stayed carefully hedged. Both Russia and China understood this and used it. Strategic ambiguity isn't a side effect of the attribution gap. It's the point.  The critical infrastructure piece was running alongside all of this. The December 2015 BlackEnergy attack on Ukraine's power grid got analyzed and formally attributed through 2016. That matters for context because before that attack, critical infrastructure intrusions were largely theoretical as a serious offensive capability. After it, they weren't. CISA's framework for thinking about 2016 election infrastructure threats was being built in real time, partly in response to watching what Russia had just done to Ukrainian power distribution. The Havex malware campaign against industrial control systems in July 2014 was precursor documentation that shaped how analysts read the threat.  The doctrinal integration is the thing that doesn't get enough attention. Russia's 2016 operations weren't a cyber program that also happened to do influence work, or an influence program that also happened to use hacked material. The Coro Cybersecurity analysis of the period describes Russia explicitly as a "cyber warfare actor" whose operations fused the two. CISA's advisory architecture was partly built to help defenders understand that you can't treat network intrusion and information warfare as separate problems when the adversary is running them as one.  What emerged institutionally from 2016 was a belated recognition that resilience planning had to account for state-level actors with both technical capability and strategic patience. The SearchInform assessment of state-backed attacks from this period points to "resilience and business continuity planning" as the core response framework that developed. That sounds bureaucratic. It is bureaucratic. It's also the honest answer when your adversaries have nation-state resources and you're trying to defend election systems that were never designed with that threat model in mind.  The 2016 period stands as a documented high-water mark in concurrent state-sponsored cyber activity against U.S. targets, per CISA's own advisory record. Two major powers, running separate operations, for separate strategic purposes, simultaneously, against overlapping target sets. The Russia story consumed the public narrative. The China story sat in the dataset.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations #CyberEspionage #CriticalInfrastructureMapping #ElectionInterference #FalseFlagAttribution #PersistentNetworkInfiltration #VulnerabilityScanning

sample_tweets.text: The vultures have started circling India's manufacturing story.  Yes, the cyberattack on Tata Electronics is serious. Sensitive Apple iPhone 18 Pro supplier documents, component details and internal files were reportedly stolen by a ransomware group and are now under investigation by Indian authorities. Tata has launched a forensic audit, while Apple and the government are investigating the breach.  But here's what many conveniently forget.  Cyberattacks don't happen only in India.  China has seen repeated cyber incidents involving manufacturers, technology firms and supply chains over the years. The US, Japan, Germany and South Korea have all suffered major industrial cyber breaches. That's the unfortunate reality of a hyper-connected global manufacturing ecosystem.  No country says, "Stop investing here because of one cyberattack."  Instead, they strengthen cybersecurity, investigate the breach, fix the vulnerabilities and move forward.  India should do exactly that.  Don't let one incident overshadow the bigger picture. Global companies are expanding manufacturing in India because of talent, scale, policy support and supply-chain diversification—not because they expect a cyberattack-free world.  Criticise security lapses. Demand accountability. Improve cyber defences.  But don't celebrate every setback as proof that India's manufacturing ambitions have failed.  The real test of a manufacturing powerhouse is not whether it faces attacks.  It's how quickly it recovers, learns and comes back stronger.

Top 10 Countries With the Most Notorious Hackers 🌍💻. Check for your Country   Russia 🇷🇺 – Known for ransomware gangs and financially motivated cybercrime groups. China 🇨🇳 – Strong state-backed cyber espionage capabilities. North Korea 🇰🇵 – Notorious for theft-focused operations, including cryptocurrency heists. Iran 🇮🇷 – Active in espionage and disruptive cyber campaigns. United States 🇺🇸 – Home to many elite hackers, though most work legally; some criminal groups also originate there. Ukraine 🇺🇦 – Has produced several well-known cybercriminal groups. Romania 🇷🇴 – Historically associated with carding and fraud operations. Brazil 🇧🇷 – Notable for banking malware and online fraud. India 🇮🇳 – Large talent pool, with some involvement in scams and cybercrime. Nigeria 🇳🇬 – Known primarily for online fraud and phishing schemes rather than highly advanced hacking.

NORTH KOREA: THREAT ACTOR PROFILE  Ninety-one documented incidents. Thirty years of operations. The pattern has not changed much, which is its own kind of message.  Pyongyang runs what is essentially a hybrid criminal-intelligence program. The revenue side and the espionage side are not separate concerns. They fund each other, they share infrastructure, and increasingly they share personnel. The IT worker infiltration schemes that U.S. counterintelligence has been flagging for the past two years are a good illustration of this: the same operative collecting a Western tech company paycheck under a false identity is also positioned to exfiltrate proprietary data. Two functions, one asset, zero overhead from Pyongyang's perspective.  North Korea is not a sophisticated actor in the way that China's APT programs are sophisticated. They don't need to be. They are relentless, they are willing to run long operations that most actors would find uneconomical, and sanctions pressure means cryptocurrency theft isn't a side hustle. It's a budget line.  ---  THE NUMBERS  Our archive covers September 1996 through June 2026. Ninety-one documented incidents. The distribution is not uniform across that span. Activity has accelerated sharply in the back half, which tracks with Pyongyang's expanded offensive cyber investment after the Sony hack era and with the explosion of cryptocurrency as a target-rich environment.  Top methods by incident count:  Cyber Espionage: 42 incidents. The plurality of their documented activity, and probably the most underreported category because successful espionage by definition leaves less of a visible trail.  Advanced Persistent Threat Operations: 30. This overlaps with the espionage category but represents the more structured, long-dwell intrusion campaigns. Lazarus Group. Kimsuky. APT38. These are not anonymous collectives. They have organizational continuity, consistent tooling, and identifiable tradecraft.  Malware Distribution and Critical Infrastructure Mapping: 14 each. The infrastructure mapping number should get more attention than it does. Mapping isn't an attack. It's preparation for an attack, and the lag between reconnaissance and use can be years.  Infrastructure Attacks: 13. Ransomware Campaigns: 12. Cryptocurrency Theft: 12. Supply Chain Exploitation: 12.  That last cluster is where Pyongyang's operational model becomes clearest. Ransomware generates hard currency. Cryptocurrency theft generates hard currency. Supply chain exploitation generates access that can be monetized or used for intelligence collection depending on what the mission requires. Every one of these is a revenue mechanism with an intelligence upside, or an intelligence operation with a revenue upside. The dual-use logic is consistent across all of it.  ---  MARQUEE CASES  The Kimsuky $285 million theft from the Drift platform in April 2026 is the one people are talking about right now, and it deserves that attention. Not because of the dollar figure, though $285 million in a single operation is significant even by North Korean standards. What's notable is the method. Six months of social engineering. Extended identity deception. Relationship-building over time to gain administrative access. This wasn't a technical exploit. Someone sat across from Drift employees, metaphorically speaking, for half a year before pulling the trigger. That level of patience and operational discipline doesn't materialize spontaneously. It reflects institutional investment in the human side of the operation alongside the technical side.  The IT worker infiltration picture has been filling in throughout 2025 and 2026. The April 2026 FBI assessment documents one prosecuted scheme that generated $5 million, which is almost certainly a floor, not a ceiling, on what these placements yield. The remote work environment eliminated the identity verification friction that would have made this harder to run at scale. That's not a novel observation at this point, but the FBI assessment's framing of this as "dual revenue and intelligence collection" is the part that matters operationally. Companies that discovered they had a North Korean IT worker on payroll should be asking not just what was paid out but what was accessed and copied. Those two questions have different answer sets.  The June 2026 PCAST-FBI letter on AI data center infrastructure is worth flagging separately. The letter documents North Korean exploitation of AI infrastructure through supply chain penetration, foreign investment vehicles, and the same IT worker infiltration vector. The regulatory gap it identifies, specifically the space between CFIUS review processes and AI infrastructure security requirements, is a real gap. CFIUS was designed around asset acquisition. The infiltration model doesn't require acquisition. It requires access, and access is obtainable through employment, through vendors, through subcontractors. The letter is correct that current review frameworks don't fully cover this.  The 2026 DNI Annual Threat Assessment's grouping of China, Russia, and North Korea as "converging multi-domain threats" is useful framing if read carefully. The convergence isn't coordination in any structured sense. It's more that three actors with overlapping interests in degrading U.S. institutional capacity are operating simultaneously, and the cumulative effect is different from what any single actor would produce. For North Korea specifically, the assessment flags IT worker infiltration as active and escalating. That assessment is consistent with what we're seeing in the incident data.  ---  TRADECRAFT NOTES  A few recurring characteristics worth tracking:  Long operations. The Drift heist was six months. Kimsuky's broader espionage campaigns have run years. North Korean operators are not optimizing for speed. They're optimizing for access, and they'll wait.  Credential-based entry. Across the cyber espionage and APT incidents in our archive, the preference is for valid credentials over technical exploits where possible. Spearphishing to harvest credentials, social engineering to gain access, then operating inside the environment with whatever permissions the compromised account holds. This makes detection harder and attribution slower.  Financial mission integration. This bears repeating because it's genuinely distinctive compared to other state actors. Russia's GRU or China's MSS run espionage programs that generate intelligence. North Korea runs espionage programs that also have to generate foreign currency. The Reconnaissance General Bureau, which oversees much of this, is not just an intelligence organ. It's a revenue-generating arm of the state. That organizational reality shapes the operational priorities in ways that can be surprising if you're expecting a conventional intelligence-collection model.  Supply chain as access vector. Twelve incidents involving supply chain exploitation in our archive. The AI infrastructure letter adds a new dimension here, specifically the concern about upstream compromise of AI training data and model infrastructure, which is a different and potentially harder-to-detect problem than traditional software supply chain attacks.  ---  WHAT TO WATCH  The IT worker infiltration vector is not going away. It scales easily, the barriers to entry are low, and the false identity infrastructure North Korea has built over years of running these operations is mature. Companies doing remote hiring, particularly in technology and defense-adjacent sectors, are the primary exposure surface.  The cryptocurrency targeting has become more sophisticated over time. The Drift operation's social engineering approach is a marked evolution from earlier technically-oriented heists. Expect more operations in this mode, targeting not just exchanges but DeFi platforms, custodial services, and the humans who have administrative access to them.  The AI infrastructure concern flagged in the PCAST-FBI letter is probably the forward edge of where this actor's supply chain focus is heading. If the objective is access to systems that will have increasing relevance to economic and military capability, AI infrastructure is the obvious target set.  One last thing. The Atlantic Council framing in the June 2026 analysis, that adversaries are now optimizing for institutional faith erosion rather than specific electoral outcomes, applies differently to North Korea than to Russia or China. North Korea's influence operation footprint is smaller than the other two. But the cyber and financial operations do cumulative damage to institutional confidence in ways that don't require a disinformation campaign. A $285 million crypto theft, an IT worker discovered inside a federal contractor's network, a ransomware hit on a hospital system. These erode the sense that the defensive apparatus is working. That erosion is strategic value, even when the operation's primary objective was the money.  Ninety-one incidents. One actor. Thirty years and still adding to the count.  foreigninterference.org #foreigninterference #CyberEspionage #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations #MalwareDistribution #CriticalInfrastructureMapping

🚨The SCIF 발표한 내용입니다!!!!  A-WEB: UN 지원 "선거 사기 카르텔"  A-WEB, 세계 최대 선거 기관으로 한국에 본부를 두고 있으며, 해킹된 NEC 시스템, 미루 기술 재난, 이라크 70% 실패율, 콩고민주공화국 45% 실패율, 유령 유권자, 위조 투표용지 및 의심스러운 글로벌 패턴과 연계되어 있습니다.  1.) 한국 중앙선거관리위원회(NEC)의 사이버 보안 실패:  - 2023년 NIS/KISA 공동 감사, 2023년 7월~9월, 2023년 10월 10일 결과 발표, NEC 시스템이 매우 취약하다고 판명.  - 유권자 등록 시스템이 침투되어 "유령 유권자"를 생성하거나 조기 투표자를 비투표자로 표시할 수 있음.  - 투표용지 인쇄 시스템이 공식 도장 파일 도난에 취약하여, 일치하는 QR 코드가 포함된 위조 투표용지 생성 가능.  - 투표 집계 시스템이 무단 USB 장치나 무선 연결을 통해 조작될 수 있음.  - 네트워크 분리가 미흡하고, 쉽게 추측 가능한 비밀번호, 후보자 명부 및 해외 유권자 명부 등 민감 데이터의 평문 저장.  - NEC가 자체 보안 점수를 100/100으로 평가했으나, 독립 재평가에서 31.5/100 점수.  - NEC가 한국 법에 따라 불법인 자격 미달 업체를 보안 평가에 사용.  - 이전 2년간 8회 사이버 공격을 받았으며, 그중 7회가 북한의 라자루스 그룹으로 추정.  - 2021년 4월, NEC 인터넷 PC가 북한 김수키 그룹의 맬웨어에 감염되어 기밀 문서 유출.  - NEC가 NIS로부터 보고된 여러 북한 해킹 사건에 대해 "사전 지식 없음".  2.) 미루 시스템 문제. NEC의 주요 기술 공급업체:  - 미루 시스템즈, 한국 성남, 한국의 전자 투표/집계 시스템 유지보수 및 수리, 국제 수출.  - 이라크 70% 오작동률, 콩고민주공화국 45% 오작동률.  - 미루 시스템을 사용하는 국가들(이라크, 콩고민주공화국, 필리핀, 키르기스스탄, 엘살바도르, 에콰도르, 불가리아)에서 심각한 사기 혐의와 시위 발생 보고.  - 2009년부터 바우만 모스크바 국립공대와의 장기적 러시아 선거 시스템 연계, 2024년 러시아 선거 포함.  - 2017년 이라크 투표 집계기 1억 달러 수출 계약.  - 미시간 대학교 교수 알렉스 할더먼: "미루의 보고된 실적은 심각한 우려를 불러일으킵니다…"  - 폴리티코 조사에서 전문가들이 미루의 실적을 "길고, 우려스럽고, 잘 문서화된" 것으로 지칭.  2024년 입법선거에서 야당에 유리한 조기 투표 결과의 디지털 조작 혐의. 윤석열 대통령이 선거 사기를 부분적 이유로 계엄령 선포, 군대가 잠시 NEC 본부 점거. 윤석열은 2026년 2월 "반란"으로 종신형 선고 받음.  조기 투표와 당일 투표 간 지속적인 통계 이상, 연구자 공병호 박사가 일관된 조작 패턴으로 묘사.  2025년 6월 3일 대통령 선거, 자유당 후보 이재명 "당선" 49.4%. 국제선거감시팀(IEMT), 전 미 대사 모스 H. 탄, 존 R. 밀스 대령(퇴역), 브래들리 A. 세이어 박사, 그랜트 뉴섬 대령(퇴역) 포함, 보고서:  - 조기 및 당일 투표 간 상당한 통계 불균형.  - 전자 집계 시스템의 보안/투명성 우려.  - 투표용지 취급 및 보관 체계의 불규칙성.  - 시민 감시자 방해 및 NEC 협조 부족.  2026년 6월 3일 지방선거, 14,288개 투표소 중 50곳에서 투표용지 부족, 22곳에서 투표 중단. NEC 위원장 노태악 사임, 경찰이 NEC 본부 급습.  A-WEB = 111개국 121개 선거관리기구, 2013년 설립, 한국 인천 송도 본부. USAID, IFES, Democracy International, NDI, IRI 등과 MOU 체결(2014).  A-WEB는 한국 선거 방법론과 기술(취약한 미루 시스템 포함)을 개발도상국에 수출하는 "UN 지원 선거 시스템 카르텔" 역할을 하며, USAID가 선거 자금을 지원하는 곳. 즉: 정권 교체 작전의 일부.  A-WEB는 2016년 현장, 2020년 가상으로 미국 선거 관찰. 상세 공식 보고서 미공개. 미국 법조인 시드니 파월이 A-WEB의 해외 활동에 대한 DOJ 조사 촉구, 한국 수원에 위치한 "국제 선거 문제 네트워크"로 묘사.  A-WEB는 이라크, 콩고민주공화국 등 미국 납세자 자금 선거의 위험 중심에 있음. A-WEB는 USAID와 연결된 주요 자금 지원 기관으로, 전 세계 정권 교체 작전을 위한 선거 조작 수단을 자금 지원하고 조직. 이는 세계 전역에 공산주의를 확산시키는 더 큰 작전의 한 부분입니다.  문서: 선거 범죄 수사국

sample_tweets.text: 🔬 HacksGuard — High-Performance TUI Malware Analysis for PE Files  HacksGuard is an open-source malware analysis tool built in Rust that delivers fast, multi-threaded static analysis of Portable Executable (PE) files through an interactive Terminal User Interface (TUI). Designed for SOC analysts, threat hunters, incident responders, and reverse engineers, it combines deep PE inspection with concurrent analysis pipelines to provide rapid malware triage without sacrificing responsiveness. The tool performs comprehensive executable analysis, including PE header inspection, section analysis, import and export enumeration, security mitigation checks (ASLR, DEP, CFG), Authenticode verification, overlay detection, entropy calculation, suspicious string extraction, and integrated VirusTotal lookups. Powered by the Boreal YARA engine, HacksGuard supports local YARA rule scanning for detecting known malware families, packers, and evasion techniques, while its heuristic engine generates an intuitive 0–100% risk score based on multiple behavioral indicators such as entropy, suspicious APIs, PE anomalies, strings, and packing characteristics. Analysts can further investigate binaries using the built-in x86/x64 disassembler powered by iced-x86, inspect raw bytes through an integrated hex viewer, visualize entropy distributions with sparklines, and automatically decode Base64-like strings directly within the interface. Beyond its interactive dashboard, HacksGuard also supports JSON output for automated pipelines, enabling seamless integration with SIEM, SOAR, CI/CD workflows, and malware triage automation systems.  📌 Credit: @0xcoili 🔗 github.com/Rhacknarok/hacksg… #CyberSecurity #MalwareAnalysis #ThreatHunting #SOC #ReverseEngineering #Rust #YARA #StaticAnalysis #BlueTeam #ThreatIntelligence

The GitHub orlofv/Cyber-Threat-Intelligence repository is a resource directory designed as a learning and reference guide for cyber threat analysts.   It centralises practical tools, data flows and essential theoretical concepts like:  Data flow and IoT: It brings together links to malware analysis platforms and compromise indicators (IoCs) such as abuse dot ch (Malware Bazaar, ThreatFox), VirusTotal and AlienVault OTX.   Reference frameworks and standards: The repository documents essential information exchange structures such as STIX/TAXII (developed by MITRE), the Diamond model and the TLP protocol.   Threat modelling: It provides comparative guides on risk and vulnerability assessment methodologies, including STRIDE, PASTA and DREAD.   Information analysis methods: The project includes Definitions and data sheets on the cognitive processes of intelligence, such as the Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) life cycle, the analysis of competing hypotheses (ACH) and the OODA loop.  github.com/orlofv/Cyber-Thre…

🛡️ Un CAPTCHA falso puede hacer que un empleado instale malware  Muchos ataques empiezan con una pantalla conocida.  Puede ser un “demuestra que eres humano”, un error falso o una instrucción urgente.  El riesgo surge cuando la página pide copiar y pegar un comando en Windows.  ⚠️ ¿Qué pasó?  El investigador Bert-Jan Pals analizó cerca de 3,000 cargas activas de ClickFix.  ClickFix es una técnica que engaña al usuario para ejecutar malware por su cuenta.  La página muestra un CAPTCHA falso y copia una instrucción maliciosa al portapapeles.  Luego pide abrir Windows Terminal o una ventana de ejecución y pegar el comando.  Según la investigación, los atacantes ya usan servidores que generan comandos distintos para cada visitante.  Eso complica la detección tradicional.  También usan archivos descargados en la carpeta Downloads para ocultar mejor el malware.  Si un empleado puede infectar su equipo siguiendo instrucciones que parecen normales.  El impacto puede llegar a robo de contraseñas, acceso a correo, ransomware o fraude con clientes.  💡 ¿Qué deben hacer?  Capaciten al equipo para nunca pegar instrucciones en Terminal o Ejecutar.  Monitoreen PowerShell, cmd y msiexec abriéndose desde navegador o descargas.  Usen AV+ con IA que prevenga amenazas antes de ejecutarse, incluso sin internet.  Complementen con EDR+ con ML de comportamiento y contexto.

🚨 CYBER INTELLIGENCE ALERT: GOVERNMENT SECTOR — INDONESIA 🇮🇩  [STATUS: ALLEGED COMPROMISE OF CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE / UNCONFIRMED / SOURCE: UNDERGROUND MONITORING COMMUNITY / DATE: JULY 3, 2026]  THE ACTOR "THESWEETNIGHT" CLAIMS ZERO-DAY INTRUSION INTO THE NATIONAL CYBERSECURITY AND CRYPTOGRAPHY AGENCY (BSSN)  Through technical incident monitoring and the interception of signals on threat actor broadcast channels, a geopolitical and cyber alert is issued. The threat actor identified under the alias TheSweetNight claims to have compromised the core systems of the Indonesian National Cybersecurity and Cryptography Agency (BSSN — Badan Siber dan Sandi Negara).  The attacker claims to have disabled or forcibly shut down 19,000 network monitoring sensors distributed throughout Indonesia. He accompanied his claim with an explicit political warning using the hashtag.  🏢 Allegedly Affected Entity: Indonesian National Cybersecurity and Cryptography Agency (BSSN)  👤 Threat Actor: TheSweetNight  ⚔️ Technical Vector Used: Exploitation of an apparent zero-day pre-authentication (Pre-Auth) bypass exploit targeting the PRTG Network Monitor infrastructure monitoring platform.  🔍 Verification Status: UNCONFIRMED BY THE INDONESIAN GOVERNMENT. As of July 3, 2026, neither the BSSN central administration nor Southeast Asian digital defense teams have issued any forensic contingency announcements or technical service disruption statements.  🗂️ FORENSIC ANALYSIS OF THE EXPLOIT VECTOR  According to the console log and documented perimeter interfaces, the intrusion was executed through the following technical stages:  Cookie Spoofing RSA Authentication: The attacker used an automated script written in Python (0day.py) targeting port 8686 of the corporate PRTG instance. The attack vector exploited a logical vulnerability that extracted the server's 2048-bit RSA public key to forge a token desynchronization bridge.  Evasion Payload Construction: The script generated a fake cookie formatted according to the PKCS#1 v1.5 cryptographic standard, incorporating privileged session variables. This signature was packaged and injected via an HTTP POST request directed to the edge access endpoint /public/checklogin.htm.  Access Granted as Superuser: The web server response logs (HTTP/1.1 200 OK) processed the status <status>Success</status>, allowing the gateway to validate the fake cookie as legitimate administrator authentication ([+] Authenticated as: prtgadmin / [+] Gateway login (successful).  Institutional Dashboard Compromise: The data breach exposes the JSON response of the vulnerable PRTG v17.3.32.2478 version, listing the user object objid: 100 corresponding to the primary role: "Operator Badan Siber dan Sandi Negara" with an Active status. Additional screenshots embedded in the image demonstrate full access to the logical logs of traffic sensors, alert logs, and government account configurations.  🛡️ PREVENTIVE TECHNICAL CONTAINMENT RECOMMENDATIONS (CISO / GLOBAL SOC)  🛑 Preventive Disconnection and Inspection of PRTG Instances: BSSN infrastructure teams are urgently urged to isolate any exposed PRTG Network Monitor instances at the perimeter from the internet. It is mandatory to check the access logs to the endpoint /public/checklogin.htm for concurrent POST requests that match the Exploit chain.  🔑 Forensic Audit and Cryptographic Key Pair Regeneration: Invalidate and regenerate the RSA key pair used by servers to sign and validate user session cookies, ensuring that any tokens previously forged by the attacker are immediately rendered unusable.  📊 MONITORING AND EVALUATION  Intelligence System:   🔑 analyzer.vecert.io 🔑 monitor.vecert.io #CyberSecurity #Indonesia #BSSN #PRTGNetworkMonitor #ZeroDay #CookieForgery #AuthenticationBypass #PrtgAdmin #ThreatActor #TheSweetNight #NetworkSensors #ThreatIntelligence #CyberAlert #VECERT #Infosec #UnverifiedIncident

#!/usr/bin/env node // [[day-21-38-team-sharing-data-plane]] headless dogfood — GERÇEK shipped // `@lodos/shared/dist` + GERÇEK compiled `dist-electron/team/*.js` (owner-cihazı // tam host-path) + GERÇEK derlenmiş sunucu roster-lib (verify-only ACL) + // IN-PROCESS KÖR KOORDİNATÖR (globalThis.fetch intercept → opak byte mailbox'ları). // // Tek Node process'inde host-modül singleton'ları (prisma/electron-store) // ilk DB'ye bağlanır → owner (A) TAM host-path'ten geçer; üye (B) yarısı ise // GERÇEK shipped shared-crypto + explicit prismaB ile replay edilir (B'nin host // modülleri byte-identik mantık — electron tsc=0 + [SEC-360..366] + verify-moat // M36 ile ayrıca kilitli). Kanıtlanan çalışma-zamanı özellikleri: //   1. Pure-module: merge determinizmi (kaybeden korunur) + admission + SAS-MITM //      + rehydrate — shipped dist. //   2. GERÇEK davet akışı (A host-path): davet üret → B bundle → SAS eşleşir → //      countersign; SAS-uyuşmazlık → TEAM_SAS_MISMATCH (countersign ASLA). //   3. GERÇEK section paylaşımı (A host-path): grant + nesne fan-out → kör //      koordinatör OPAK taşır; B, owner-DEK OLMADAN sealed-grant + imzalı-nesneden //      section içeriğini AÇAR (K28 "B'nin ekranına nasıl ulaşır"). //   4. Koordinatör-körlük: mailbox'lar OPAK base64 + owner-imzasız roster event //      → 403 (sunucu roster'ı DEĞİŞTİREMEZ) + source-tripwire.  import { createRequire } from 'node:module'; import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url'; import path from 'node:path'; import fs from 'node:fs'; import os from 'node:os'; import crypto from 'node:crypto';  const __file = fileURLToPath(import.meta.url); const ROOT = path.resolve(path.dirname(__file), '..'); const DESKTOP = path.join(ROOT, 'apps/desktop'); const requireDesktop = createRequire(path.join(DESKTOP, 'package.json')); const shared = requireDesktop('@lodos/shared'); const serverRoster = requireDesktop(path.join(ROOT, 'apps/sync-server/dist-test/team-roster.cjs'));  let PASS = 0; let FAIL = 0; function check(name, cond, detail = '') { if (cond) { PASS += 1; console.log(`  ✓ ${name}`); } else { FAIL += 1; console.log(`  ✗ ${name}${detail ? ` — ${detail}` : ''}`); } } function prismaFor(url) { const { PrismaClient } = requireDesktop('@prisma/client'); return new PrismaClient({ datasources: { db: { url } } }); }  // ── In-process KÖR koordinatör: server-blind opak byte mailbox'ları ── function makeCoordinator() { const channels = new Map(); const envelopes = []; const objects = []; const invites = new Map(); let idc = 0; const nid = () => `id-${(idc += 1)}`; function stateOf(ch) { if (!ch || ch.rosterSeq === 0) return null; return { seq: ch.rosterSeq, headHash: ch.rosterHead, currentOwnerFp: ch.currentOwnerFp, currentOwnerEd25519Pub: ch.currentOwnerPub, members: new Map(ch.members) }; } async function handle(url, init) { const u = new URL(url, 'http://coord'); const parts = u.pathname.split('/').filter(Boolean); const method = init?.method ?? 'GET'; const body = init?.body ? JSON.parse(init.body) : null; const json = (obj, status = 200) => ({ ok: status < 400, status, async json() { return obj; } }); if (parts[1] === 'team' && parts.length === 2 && method === 'POST') {  const d = serverRoster.evaluateRosterAppend(null, body.genesis.event, Buffer.from(body.genesis.sig, 'base64')); if (!d.ok) return json({ ok: false, error: { code: d.code } }, d.status); channels.set(body.channelId, { rosterSeq: 1, rosterHead: d.eventHash, currentOwnerFp: d.nextState.currentOwnerFp, currentOwnerPub: d.nextState.currentOwnerEd25519Pub, events: [{ seq: 1, event: d.event, sig: body.genesis.sig, eventHash: d.eventHash }], members: d.nextState.members }); return json({ ok: true, team: { channelId: body.channelId, rosterSeq: 1, rosterHead: d.eventHash } }); } const channelId = parts[2]; const ch = channels.get(channelId); const sub = parts[3]; if (sub === 'roster') { if (method === 'GET') { if (!ch) return json({ ok: false, error: { code: 'VALIDATION' } }, 404); return json({ ok: true, events: ch.events.map((e) => ({ seq: e.seq, event: e.event, sig: e.sig, eventHash: e.eventHash })) }); } const d = serverRoster.evaluateRosterAppend(stateOf(ch), body.event, Buffer.from(body.sig, 'base64')); if (!d.ok) return json({ ok: false, error: { code: d.code } }, d.status); ch.events.push({ seq: d.event.seq, event: d.event, sig: body.sig, eventHash: d.eventHash }); ch.rosterSeq = d.nextState.seq; ch.rosterHead = d.nextState.headHash; ch.currentOwnerFp = d.nextState.currentOwnerFp; ch.currentOwnerPub = d.nextState.currentOwnerEd25519Pub; ch.members = d.nextState.members; return json({ ok: true, roster: { seq: d.nextState.seq, head: d.nextState.headHash, seatCount: 1 } }); } if (sub === 'invite' && parts.length === 4) { if (method === 'POST') { invites.set(`${channelId}:${body.inviteId}`, { expiresAt: Date.now() + 3600_000, used: false, bundle: null, bundleFp: null }); return json({ ok: true, invite: { inviteId: body.inviteId, expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 3600_000).toISOString() } }); } const inv = invites.get(`${channelId}:${u.searchParams.get('inviteId')}`); if (!inv) return json({ ok: false, error: { code: 'TEAM_INVITE_EXPIRED' } }, 410); return json({ ok: true, invite: { inviteId: u.searchParams.get('inviteId'), expiresAt: new Date(inv.expiresAt).toISOString(), used: inv.used, bundle: inv.bundle, bundleFp: inv.bundleFp } }); } if (sub === 'invite' && parts[5] === 'bundle') { const inv = invites.get(`${channelId}:${parts[4]}`); if (!inv || inv.used || Date.now() >= inv.expiresAt) return json({ ok: false, error: { code: 'TEAM_INVITE_EXPIRED' } }, 410); inv.used = true; inv.bundle = body.blob; inv.bundleFp = body.fingerprint; // KÖR: parse YOK return json({ ok: true, bundle: { inviteId: parts[4], uploadedAt: new Date().toISOString() } }); } if (sub === 'envelopes') { if (method === 'POST') { const id = nid(); envelopes.push({ id, channelId, recipientFp: body.recipientFp, blob: body.blob }); return json({ ok: true, envelope: { id } }); } const rfp = u.searchParams.get('recipientFp'); const rows = envelopes.filter((e) => e.channelId === channelId && e.recipientFp === rfp); return json({ ok: true, envelopes: rows.map((e) => ({ id: e.id, blob: e.blob, createdAt: new Date().toISOString() })), nextCursor: null }); } if (sub === 'objects') { if (method === 'POST') { const id = nid(); objects.push({ id, channelId, objectKey: body.objectKey, versionSeq: body.versionSeq, authorFp: body.authorFp, blob: body.blob }); return json({ ok: true, object: { id } }); } const rows = objects.filter((o) => o.channelId === channelId); return json({ ok: true, objects: rows.map((o) => ({ id: o.id, objectKey: o.objectKey, versionSeq: o.versionSeq, authorFp: o.authorFp, blob: o.blob, createdAt: new Date().toISOString() })), nextCursor: null }); } return json({ ok: false, error: { code: 'NOT_FOUND' } }, 404); } return { handle, _envelopes: envelopes, _objects: objects, _invites: invites, _channels: channels }; } async function main() { console.log('[dogfood-day-21-38] sharing-data-plane — owner host-path + kör koordinatör
'); const devDb = path.join(DESKTOP, 'prisma/dev.db');  if (!fs.existsSync(devDb)) throw new Error('dev.db yok — önce migrate deploy'); // ── 1. Pure-module (shipped dist) ── console.log('— 1. Pure-module: merge/admission/SAS/rehydrate (shipped dist) —'); const A = await shared.generateMemberIdentity();  const B = await shared.generateMemberIdentity(); const fpA = shared.memberFingerprint(A.x25519Pub, A.ed25519Pub); const fpB = shared.memberFingerprint(B.x25519Pub, B.ed25519Pub); const mkObj = (over) => shared.SharedObjectVersionSchema.parse({ v: 1, teamId: 't', objectType: 'section', objectId: 's1', versionSeq: 1, prevVersionHash: shared.GENESIS_OBJECT_HASH, keyEpoch: 1, ciphertext: Buffer.from('c').toString('base64'), nonce: Buffer.from('n').toString('base64'), authorKeyId: `member:${fpA}`, hlc: { physicalMs: 1000, logical: 0 }, ...over }); const sign = async (o, priv) => ({ object: o, sig: new Uint8Array(await shared.signSharedObject(o, priv)) }); const base = await sign(mkObj({}), A.ed25519Priv); const baseHash = shared.sharedObjectVersionHash(base.object, base.sig); const wA = await sign(mkObj({ versionSeq: 2, prevVersionHash: baseHash, authorKeyId: `member:${fpA}`, hlc: { physicalMs: 2000, logical: 0 } }), A.ed25519Priv); const wB = await sign(mkObj({ versionSeq: 2, prevVersionHash: baseHash, authorKeyId: `member:${fpB}`, hlc: { physicalMs: 2500, logical: 0 } }), B.ed25519Priv); for (const perm of [[wB, base, wA], [wA, wB, base], [base, wA, wB]]) { const m = shared.mergeObjectVersions({ objectId: 's1', localHead: null, incoming: perm, currentKeyEpoch: 1 }); check('merge deterministik (varış-sırası bağımsız): kazanan wB + kaybeden wA KORUNUR', m.applied.length === 2 && m.conflicts.length === 1 && shared.sharedObjectVersionHash(m.conflicts[0].object, m.conflicts[0].sig) === shared.sharedObjectVersionHash(wA.object, wA.sig)); } check('K29 Phase-3a admission: section geçer, wikiPage/task → TEAM_OBJECT_TYPE_UNSUPPORTED', shared.evaluateObjectAdmission('section').ok && !shared.evaluateObjectAdmission('wikiPage').ok && !shared.evaluateObjectAdmission('task').ok); const sasOwner = shared.deriveInviteSas(fpA, fpB, 'ab'.repeat(16)); const sasMitm = shared.deriveInviteSas(fpA, 'f'.repeat(64), 'ab'.repeat(16)); check('SAS: aynı-girdi 6 kelime eşit; MITM (farklı fp) → AYRIŞIR', sasOwner.words.length === 6 && JSON.stringify(sasOwner.words) !== JSON.stringify(sasMitm.words)); const rw = shared.planRehydrate({ s1: { versionSeq: 5, versionHash: 'a'.repeat(64) } }, { s1: { versionSeq: 3, versionHash: 'b'.repeat(64) } }); check('rehydrate: replica watermark gerisinde → TEAM_REPLICA_REHYDRATE_REQUIRED', !rw.ok && rw.code === 'TEAM_REPLICA_REHYDRATE_REQUIRED'); check('K31 bundle: self-sig doğrulanır; pubkey-swap → fingerprint-recompute reddi', (await (async () => { const core = { v: 1, inviteId: 'ab'.repeat(16), teamId: 't', algo: 'x25519+ed25519', x25519Pub: Buffer.from(B.x25519Pub).toString('base64'), ed25519Pub: Buffer.from(B.ed25519Pub).toString('base64'), fingerprint: fpB, serverUserId: 'user-b', displayName: 'B' }; const ss = await shared.signPubkeyBundle(core, B.ed25519Priv); const bundle = shared.PubkeyBundleSchema.parse({ ...core, selfSig: ss.toString('base64') }); const okR = await shared.verifyPubkeyBundle(bundle); const swapped = { ...bundle, x25519Pub: Buffer.from(A.x25519Pub).toString('base64') }; const badR = await shared.verifyPubkeyBundle(swapped); return okR.ok && !badR.ok && badR.code === 'TEAM_INVITE_BUNDLE_INVALID'; })())); // ── 2-4. GERÇEK owner host-path + kör koordinatör ── console.log('
— 2-4. GERÇEK owner host-path (dist-electron) + kör koordinatör —'); const coord = makeCoordinator();  const realFetch = globalThis.fetch; globalThis.fetch = async (url, init) => coord.handle(String(url), init); const tmp = fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'lodos-2138-A-')); const db = path.join(tmp, 'work.db'); for (const ext of ['', '-wal', '-shm']) if (fs.existsSync(devDb + ext)) fs.copyFileSync(devDb + ext, db + ext);  process.env.DATABASE_URL = `file:${db}`; process.env.LODOS_STORE_CWD = tmp; const roster = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/team/roster.js')); const active = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/team/active.js')); const identity = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/team/identity.js'));  const invite = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/team/invite-flow.js')); const grants = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/team/grants.js')); const fanout = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/team/fanout.js')); const objects = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/team/objects.js')); const cfg = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/sync/config.js')); const { vaultState } = requireDesktop(path.join(DESKTOP, 'dist-electron/state/vault-state.js')); const prisma = prismaFor(process.env.DATABASE_URL); await prisma.teamSharedObject.deleteMany({}); await prisma.team.deleteMany({}); active.invalidateTeamActiveCache(); const dekA = crypto.randomBytes(32); vaultState.unlock(dekA); await cfg.saveSyncConfig({ endpoint: 'http://coord', apiToken: 'a'.repeat(64), userId: 'user-a' });  const team = await roster.createTeam('acme', dekA); const inv = await invite.createInviteFlow(team.teamId, dekA); const channelId = [...coord._channels.keys()][0];  check('A: team kuruldu + koordinatöre kayıtlı (genesis owner-imzalı verify-only kabul)', coord._channels.size === 1 && inv.code.startsWith('lodos-team1.') && coord._channels.get(channelId).rosterSeq === 1); // B (davetli) bundle'ını üret + koordinatöre yükle (B'nin host-yarısı shared+direct-coord replay) const ownerIdentity = await identity.getMemberIdentity(team.teamId, dekA); const decodedInvite = shared.decodeInviteCode(inv.code);  const bCore = { v: 1, inviteId: decodedInvite.inviteId, teamId: team.teamId, algo: 'x25519+ed25519', x25519Pub: Buffer.from(B.x25519Pub).toString('base64'), ed25519Pub: Buffer.from(B.ed25519Pub).toString('base64'), fingerprint: fpB, serverUserId: 'user-b', displayName: 'Bengi' }; const bSelfSig = await shared.signPubkeyBundle(bCore, B.ed25519Priv); const bBundle = shared.PubkeyBundleSchema.parse({ ...bCore, selfSig: bSelfSig.toString('base64') }); await coord.handle(`http://coord/api/team/${channelId}/invite/${decodedInvite.inviteId}/bundle`, { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify({ fingerprint: fpB, blob: Buffer.from(JSON.stringify(bBundle), 'utf-8').toString('base64') }) }); check('B: bundle koordinatöre yüklendi (OPAK sakland ı — parse yok)', coord._invites.get(`${channelId}:${decodedInvite.inviteId}`).used === true && coord._invites.get(`${channelId}:${decodedInvite.inviteId}`).bundle !== null); // A: bundle geldi mi + SAS eşleşiyor mu (owner host-path) const fetched = await invite.fetchInviteBundleFlow(team.teamId, inv.inviteId, dekA); const bView = shared.deriveInviteSas(ownerIdentity.fingerprint, fpB, inv.inviteId);  check('A: bundle READY + SAS kelimeleri iki tarafta AYNI (koordinatör-MITM olsa ayrışırdı)', fetched.status === 'ready' && fetched.fingerprint === fpB && JSON.stringify(fetched.sasWords) === JSON.stringify(bView.words)); // negatif: SAS reddi → countersign ASLA let sasRejected = false; try { await invite.countersignInviteFlow(team.teamId, inv.inviteId, false, dekA); }  catch (e) { sasRejected = /TEAM_SAS_MISMATCH/.test(e.message); } check('A: SAS-uyuşmazlık → countersign REDDEDİLDİ (TEAM_SAS_MISMATCH; roster DEĞİŞMEDİ)', sasRejected && coord._channels.get(channelId).rosterSeq === 1); // pozitif: countersign → member_add + push const cs = await invite.countersignInviteFlow(team.teamId, inv.inviteId, true, dekA); check('A: SAS onayı → member_add commit + roster push (koordinatör owner-imzasını verify-only doğruladı → seq=2)',  cs.fingerprint === fpB && coord._channels.get(channelId).rosterSeq === 2); // A: section yaz → grant + nesne fan-out const enc = await shared.encryptSection({ token: 'ghp_owner_secret_XYZ' }, dekA); const sec = await prisma.secretSection.create({ data: {  name: 'shared-github', templateId: 'github', encryptedJson: enc.encryptedJson, nonce: enc.nonce, keyWrapped: enc.keyWrapped, nonSensitive: '{}', sensitiveKeys: '["token"]', fieldSchema: '[]', productionTagged: false, keyEpoch: 1 } }); await grants.grantSectionToMember({ teamId: team.teamId, sectionId: sec.id, memberFingerprint: fpB }, dekA); await objects.produceSectionObject(team.teamId, sec.id, dekA); await fanout.pushGrantEnvelopes(team.teamId, dekA); await objects.pushPendingObjects(team.teamId); check('A: section paylaşıldı → 1 grant-zarfı + 1 nesne koordinatöre fan-out (ikisi de OPAK base64)', coord._envelopes.length === 1 && coord._objects.length === 1 && /^[A-Za-z0-9+/]+=*$/.test(coord._envelopes[0].blob) && /^[A-Za-z0-9+/]+=*$/.test(coord._objects[0].blob)); // ── B tarafı: owner-DEK OLMADAN section'ı AÇ (shared-crypto replay) ── console.log('
— B (üye) owner-DEK OLMADAN paylaşılan section'ı açar (shared replay) —'); const dekB = crypto.randomBytes(32); // B roster'ı BAĞIMSIZ doğrular (owner-DEK YOK). const remoteRoster = await coord.handle(`http://coord/api/team/${channelId}/roster`, {});  const rj = await remoteRoster.json(); const signedEvents = rj.events.map((e) => ({ event: shared.RosterEventSchema.parse(e.event), sig: new Uint8Array(Buffer.from(e.sig, 'base64')) })); const genesisPub = Buffer.from(signedEvents[0].event.member.ed25519Pub, 'base64'); const chain = await shared.verifyRosterChain(signedEvents, genesisPub); check('B: roster BAĞIMSIZ doğrulandı (verifyRosterChain OK; owner-fp SAS-anchor ile eşleşir)', chain.ok && chain.ownerFingerprint === ownerIdentity.fingerprint && chain.members.size === 2); // B: mailbox'tan sealed grant-zarfını aç (kendi keypair'iyle — owner-DEK YOK). const myEnvs = await coord.handle(`http://coord/api/team/${channelId}/envelopes?recipientFp=${fpB}`, {}); const ej = await myEnvs.json(); const grantEnv = shared.SectionGrantEnvelopeSchema.parse( await shared.openTeamEnvelope(Buffer.from(ej.envelopes[0].blob, 'base64'), B.x25519Pub, B.x25519Priv));  const sectionKeyB = await shared.unwrapSectionKeyWithMember(Buffer.from(grantEnv.wrappedKey, 'base64'), B.x25519Pub, B.x25519Priv); check('B: sealed grant-zarfını KENDİ keypair'iyle açtı → sectionKey elde etti (owner-DEK YOK)', sectionKeyB.length === 32); // B: imzalı nesneyi çek → yazar-imzasını doğrula → payload'ı aç → içerik. const myObjs = await coord.handle(`http://coord/api/team/${channelId}/objects`, {}); const oj = await myObjs.json(); const wire = JSON.parse(Buffer.from(oj.objects[0].blob, 'base64').toString('utf-8')); const obj = shared.SharedObjectVersionSchema.parse(wire.object);  const objSig = new Uint8Array(Buffer.from(wire.sig, 'base64')); const authorFp = obj.authorKeyId.slice('member:'.length); const authorPub = Buffer.from(chain.members.get(authorFp).ed25519PubB64, 'base64'); const sigOk = await shared.verifySharedObjectSig(obj, objSig, new Uint8Array(authorPub)); const payload = shared.SectionObjectPayloadSchema.parse(await shared.openObjectPayload(obj.ciphertext, obj.nonce, sectionKeyB)); const innerPlain = await shared.decryptSectionContent( new Uint8Array(Buffer.from(payload.encryptedJson, 'base64')), new Uint8Array(Buffer.from(payload.nonce, 'base64')), sectionKeyB); check('B: imzalı-nesne yazar-imzası doğrulandı + payload açıldı + section içeriği owner-DEK OLMADAN OKUNDU', sigOk && innerPlain.token === 'ghp_owner_secret_XYZ'); sectionKeyB.fill(0); void dekB; // ── Koordinatör-körlük çalışma-zamanı + kaynak-tripwire ── console.log('
— Koordinatör-körlük (çalışma-zamanı + kaynak) —'); const forged = shared.RosterEventSchema.parse({ v: 1, type: 'member_add', teamId: 't', seq: 2, prevHash: 'a'.repeat(64), at: '2026-07-04T00:00:00.000Z', member: { x25519Pub: Buffer.from(B.x25519Pub).toString('base64'), ed25519Pub: Buffer.from(B.ed25519Pub).toString('base64'), displayName: 'B', fingerprint: fpB } });  const dec = serverRoster.evaluateRosterAppend( { seq: 1, headHash: 'a'.repeat(64), currentOwnerFp: fpA, currentOwnerEd25519Pub: Buffer.from(A.ed25519Pub).toString('base64'), members: new Map() }, forged, new Uint8Array(await shared.signRosterEvent(forged, B.ed25519Priv))); check('koordinatör: owner-imzasız (üye-imzalı) roster event → 403 TEAM_ROSTER_SIG_INVALID (sunucu roster'ı DEĞİŞTİREMEZ)', !dec.ok && dec.code === 'TEAM_ROSTER_SIG_INVALID' && dec.status === 403); const objRouteSrc = fs.readFileSync(path.join(ROOT, 'apps/sync-server/src/app/api/team/[channel]/objects/route.ts'), 'utf8'); check('objects route source-tripwire: crypto/@lodos-shared import YOK + JSON.parse(blob) YOK + DELETE YOK + OPAK beyan + ACL', !/libsodium|crypto_box|@lodos/shared/.test(objRouteSrc) && !/JSON\.parse\([^)]*blob/i.test(objRouteSrc) && !objRouteSrc.includes('export async function DELETE') && objRouteSrc.includes('OPAK') && objRouteSrc.includes('teamMemberGate')); const migSql = fs.readFileSync(path.join(ROOT, 'apps/sync-server/prisma/migrations/20260704030000_team_sharing_data_plane/migration.sql'), 'utf8'); check('migration additive: tek ADD COLUMN (Subscription.teamChannelId nullable) + CREATE TABLE/INDEX + BYTEA opak; DROP/mevcut-tablo-mutasyonu YOK', (migSql.match(/ADD COLUMN/g) || []).length === 1 && /ADD COLUMN "teamChannelId" TEXT;/.test(migSql) && /"blob" BYTEA NOT NULL/.test(migSql) && /"bundleBlob" BYTEA/.test(migSql) && !/DROP TABLE|DROP COLUMN|NOT NULL DEFAULT/.test(migSql.replace(/CREATE TABLE[\s\S]*/, ''))); globalThis.fetch = realFetch; await prisma.$disconnect(); console.log(`
[dogfood-day-21-38] SONUÇ: ${PASS} PASS / ${FAIL} FAIL`); if (FAIL > 0) process.exit(1); } main().catch((e) => { console.error('
[dogfood-day-21-38] FATAL:', e); process.exit(1); });

sample_tweets.text: It seems my vulnerability is often met with prolonged silence. I don’t know why I still find it difficult to accept that silence is, in itself, a response albeit not the one that I hoped for.

Pablo, what moves me most about this isn't just your vulnerability—it's the way you've redefined what leadership truly means. For so long, you've carried stories, hopes, and dreams that gave others the strength to keep going. Yet this journal reminds us that even those who inspire millions are still learning to extend that same grace to themselves.  "I can't lead people to the sky while denying myself the right to what I'm still becoming." That may be one of the most profound things you've ever written. It reminds us that real leadership isn't built on self-erasure or endless sacrifice. It's built on the courage to grow alongside the people you're leading.  Thank you for showing us that choosing yourself isn't an act of selfishness—it is an act of stewardship. Because the light you've always encouraged others to see within themselves deserves to shine just as brightly in you. Perhaps that's what "returning to yourself" truly means: not becoming someone new, but finally believing the same truths you've spent years helping the rest of us believe about ourselves.
